label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I have this problem here about a chessboard with two opposite corners removed and whether it can be tiled with dominoes. Let me try to figure this out step by step. First, I know that a regular 8x8 chessboard has 64 squares. If we remove two diagonally opposite corners, we’re left with 62 squares. The question is, can these 62 squares be perfectly covered by 31 dominoes, each covering two adjacent squares? Hmm, domino tiling problems often involve some kind of parity or coloring argument. The classic chessboard coloring comes to mind—alternating black and white squares. Let me recall that each domino, when placed on a chessboard, will always cover one black and one white square, right? Because they’re adjacent, and adjacent squares on a chessboard are always different colors. So, if the original chessboard has an equal number of black and white squares, which it does—32 black and 32 white—removing two opposite corners... wait, what color are those corners? Diagonally opposite corners on a chessboard are the same color. For example, the top-left corner is usually a dark square, and the bottom-right corner, being diagonal, is also dark. Similarly, the other two corners are light. So if we remove two dark squares, the remaining squares would have 30 dark and 32 light squares, or vice versa? Let me check that. Suppose
